entries.notes: alsoKnownAs: Sand Sifter Star, Sand Star, Burrowing Sea Star. Indo-Pacific bottom-dweller that sifts sand for detritus and microfauna; often added for cleanup. Notable caveat: in smaller tanks (under ~280 L) it commonly DEPLETES the deep-sand-bed microfauna and slowly starves. Flag this in careNotes. Not strictly reef-safe — bulldozes sand and may topple frags.

alsoKnownAs: Blue Star, Comet Star, Comet Sea Star. Notoriously high mortality in captivity — most specimens die within 6-12 months from slow starvation (specialized microbial film diet that's hard to replicate). Specialist-only — flag this prominently in careNotes. Requires very long acclimation (3+ hours drip), large mature tank (>300 L) with extensive live rock microfauna. Set hobbyistAdvisory: specialist-only.

alsoKnownAs: Multi-colored Linckia, Orange Linckia, Mottled Linckia, Comet Sea Star, Tamaria spp. (some trade), Linckia guildingii (Caribbean congener — folded in as alsoKnownAs per hybrid policy). Smaller, more variably-colored congener of L. laevigata; same care difficulty (specialist-only — high mortality from slow starvation).

alsoKnownAs: Tile Star, Red Tile Star, Red Sea Star (trade), Brick Red Sea Star. Smaller and somewhat hardier than Linckia — feeds on microfilm and detritus. Reef safe. Still considered moderate-difficulty (better acclimation tolerance than Linckia, but still vulnerable to slow starvation in too-clean systems).
alsoKnownAs: Marbled Sea Star, Mottled Fromia, Indian Sea Star, Marbled Fromia. Indo-Pacific Fromia with brick-red and white mottling. Care parallels F. milleporella. Reef safe.
alsoKnownAs: Necklace Starfish, Tiled Sea Star, Red Tile Star (trade overlap with F. milleporella). Striking red-and-white reticulated pattern. Reef safe. Same general Fromia care.

alsoKnownAs: Chocolate Chip Starfish, Horned Sea Star, Knobbed Sea Star (trade overlap with Protoreaster linckii), Nodose Sea Star. NOT reef safe — eats coral polyps, clams, soft corals, and small invertebrates. Best for FOWLR (fish-only-with-live-rock) systems. Hardier than Linckia and Fromia, often considered a starter sea star, but the reef-incompatibility is the key caveat.

alsoKnownAs: Red-Knob Starfish, African Red Knob Sea Star, Banded Sea Star. African/Indian Ocean congener of the Chocolate Chip (P. nodosus). Same NOT-reef-safe caveat — eats sessile invertebrates. Bright orange-red with raised dark knobs. Best for FOWLR. Aqua Imports carries this distinct from the Indo-Pacific P. nodosus.

alsoKnownAs: Tiger Serpent Star, Tiger Striped Serpent Star, Brittle Star (broad trade). Caribbean serpent star with distinctive orange-and-cream banded pattern. Reef safe — scavenges detritus and uneaten food. Smooth arms (not bristly like Ophiocoma); hides in rockwork. Considered one of the easier brittle/serpent stars to keep.

alsoKnownAs: Green Serpent Star, Emerald Brittle Star. NOTORIOUS for actively hunting and eating small fish at night — uses the umbrella ambush technique. NOT safe with cardinalfish, gobies, or other small/slow fish. Reef safe to corals but flag the fish-predation caveat very prominently in careNotes.

alsoKnownAs: Red Bristle Star, Black Brittle Star (color variants), Spiny Brittle Star, Macrophiothrix sp. (common trade confusion). Bristly-armed Indo-Pacific brittle star. Scavenges detritus and uneaten food. Reef safe. Hides during the day, active at night. Distinct from the smoother-armed Ophiolepis serpent stars.

alsoKnownAs: Black Long-spine Urchin, Diadema antillarum (Caribbean congener — folded in via alsoKnownAs per hybrid policy; care effectively identical). VENOMOUS spines deliver painful stings if handled. Reef safe — primarily grazes algae and films. Topples rockwork in smaller tanks. Set hobbyistAdvisory: specialist-only ONLY if the venom is severe enough to warrant a public-facing warning.

alsoKnownAs: Sphere Urchin, Globe Urchin, Halloween Urchin (orange-and-black color morph), Blue Tuxedo Urchin (alternate color morph). Compact reef-safe urchin that grazes algae and films. Most popular ornamental urchin in the hobby. Decorates itself with shell fragments, rubble, and frags (frag-theft caveat). Color morphs (Blue/Halloween/Red) covered by the hybrid policy in a single entry.

alsoKnownAs: Slate Pencil Urchin, Slate Urchin, Red Pencil Urchin. Indo-Pacific urchin with thick blunt 'pencil' spines. Reef safe but may topple frags and pick at corallines. Algae and biofilm grazer. Considered one of the hardier urchin species in the hobby.

alsoKnownAs: Green Sea Urchin, Variegated Urchin, Caribbean Pincushion. Caribbean species often added for algae and cyanobacteria control. Reef safe. Decorates with shell fragments. Better behaved (smaller, less destructive) than the larger Echinothrix or pencil urchins.

alsoKnownAs: Shortspine Urchin, Burrowing Urchin, Boring Urchin, Echinometra lucunter (Caribbean congener — folded in). Indo-Pacific rock-grinding urchin that excavates burrows in live rock — flag this destructive habit in careNotes. Algae grazer.

alsoKnownAs: Tigertail Cucumber, Tiger Tail, Striped Sea Cucumber. Caribbean detritivore reaching ~1 m in length. Common in mature reef setups for sand cleanup. Holothurians release saponin toxins when stressed/dying — flag the tank-wipeout caveat (less severe than Sea Apple but still real).

alsoKnownAs: Pink Cucumber, Edible Sea Cucumber, Black Sea Cucumber (color variant). Reef-safe Indo-Pacific detritivore that processes sand bed. Same saponin-toxin caveat as other Holothuria. Sandbed depletion warning for smaller tanks.

alsoKnownAs: Australian Sea Apple, Violet Sea Apple. Filter-feeding sea cucumber with striking violet body, yellow oral feeding tentacles. Eats microscopic phytoplankton (target-fed phyto cultures). NOTORIOUS TANK-KILLER RISK: when stressed or dying, releases potent saponin toxin (holothurin) that can kill all fish and inverts in the system. Set hobbyistAdvisory: specialist-only with the toxin-risk reason. Some reefers refuse to keep them entirely.
alsoKnownAs: Lollyfish, Black Cucumber. Reef-safe Indo-Pacific detritivore. Often gets very large (~60 cm). Saponin-toxin caveat applies. Less common in the hobby than H. edulis.

alsoKnownAs: Sea Strawberry, Red/Pink Filter-Feeding Cucumber, Colochirus quadrangularis (sometimes traded as same). Small (~5 cm) filter-feeding sea cucumber popular in nano reefs. Eats phytoplankton (similar to Sea Apple but smaller, lower toxin risk). Reef safe.

alsoKnownAs: Crinoid, Feather Crinoid, Heterometra savignyi (common trade alt), Comatella stelligera. Specialty crinoid — VERY high captive mortality. Filter feeder requiring constant water-column phytoplankton; almost always slowly starves in standard reef systems. Set hobbyistAdvisory: specialist-only with the high-mortality reason. Genus-level umbrella entry — the trade often sells these as 'Feather Star' or 'Crinoid' without species ID.
